--- author: - name: "Christina Muedsam" date: last-modified number-sections: false execute: warning: false message: false --- # Project Hail Mary {.unnumbered} *DNAzyme-like motifs, horizontal gene transfer, and short linear motif conservation in Potyviridae* [⚑ preprint — not peer reviewed]{.preprint-badge} ::: {.column-margin} **⚑ Preprint — not peer reviewed** This compendium is under active development and has not been peer reviewed. Much of it is exploratory, and several of the central ideas (in particular the DNAzyme-activity, horizontal-gene-transfer, and AP-4 / tyrosine-motif immune hypotheses) are presented as explicit, testable hypotheses rather than established results — they are flagged as such throughout. Analyses, figures, interpretations and conclusions may change; please treat everything here as a living draft and cite with that caveat. ::: ## Overview This compendium documents the discovery and characterisation of a DNAzyme-like catalytic domain sequence that maps precisely to the genomes of multiple Potyviridae species, with flanking regions matching eukaryotic genes across plant, fungal, and animal kingdoms. Cross-kingdom horizontal gene transfer is the most parsimonious explanation for this pattern. Additionally, the Potyviridae CI helicase - harbouring the DNAzyme-like sequence, corresponds to a conserved protein motif likely involved in viral-host interactions associated with intracellular sorting (potentially via AP-4), viral replication, viral spread and immunity. ### Key findings at a glance - A DNAzyme catalytic domain sequence matches perfectly to a region in the genomes of several potyviruses (6 species, 16 isolates). - The flanking regions of those DNAzyme-matching sequences (16 isolates, 9 identical by sequence) are identical to regions in the genomes of several eukaryotes, including plants (potential viral hosts) and insects (potential viral vectors). - Interactions between those eukaryotic species suggest the homology might reflect cross-kingdom horizontal DNA transfer. - The eukaryotic genes span diverse functions — DNA repair, transcription regulation, chromatin remodelling, RNA processing, translation, and genes of unknown function — several of them essential for cell survival and development. - The CI protein also carries conserved short linear motifs (ELM) beyond the DNAzyme-matching sequence itself, including an accessible tyrosine-based (YXXΦ) sorting signal alongside an acidic-dileucine AP-2 motif, recurring with genus-specific variation across isolates. - Those sorting motifs motivate a testable hypothesis that the CI protein co-opts host intracellular trafficking — potentially via the AP-4 adaptor complex — to aid viral replication and spread, and to perturb the same trafficking machinery used in plant immune signalling. ### Explore by thread The work spans multiple interconnected research threads, each running through several chapters. Drag a node to rearrange, hover to trace its connections, click a chapter to open it. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` Interactive map of five analysis threads and the chapters that investigate them. Drag to rearrange, hover to trace connections, click a chapter node to open it. ::: ::: Prefer a plain list? The chapter table below covers the same ground. The work spans several interconnected research threads: | Chapter | Topic | Key tools | |---|---|---| | [1 — Discovery](notebooks/01_discovery.qmd) | DNAzyme match + eukaryotic homologs | BLAST, Biostrings | | [2 — Phylogenetics](notebooks/02_phylogenetics.qmd) | CI protein tree, motif mapping | MAFFT, IQ-TREE 2, ape | | [3 — Codon Usage](notebooks/03_codon_dnazyme.qmd) | RSCU, ENC, host adaptation, DNAzyme locus | potytools, seqinr | | [4 — ELM Motifs](notebooks/04_protein_motifs.qmd) | Short linear motif conservation in CI | ELM REST API, potytools | | [5 — Potyvirus Motifs](notebooks/05_potyvirus_motifs.qmd) | CI 3D structure, motif catalogue, interaction network | AlphaFold, igraph, visNetwork | | [6 — HGT](notebooks/06_hgt.qmd) | Phylogenetic incongruence, sequence logos | ggseqlogo, potytools | | [7 — Networks](notebooks/07_networks.qmd) | Host-virus ecological networks | igraph, BioNexus app | | [8 — Immunity](notebooks/08_immu_simmu.qmd) | PTI/ETI simulation under genetic perturbations | Observable JS | | [9 — AP-4 Trafficking](notebooks/09_ap4_trafficking.qmd) | AP-4/tyrosine-motif immune-trafficking hypothesis | — | ## Reproducibility All computationally intensive steps (alignment, phylogenetic inference, NCBI fetching, ELM queries) are orchestrated by a `targets` pipeline: ```r # Install the pipeline dependencies once install.packages(c("targets", "tarchetypes")) remotes::install_local("packages/potytools") # Run the full pipeline (skips up-to-date steps automatically) targets::tar_make() # Visualise dependencies targets::tar_visnetwork() ``` External tools required on `PATH` for running the entire pipeline: `mafft`, `iqtree2`, `hmmscan`. ## Interactive Companion App The **BioNexus** Shiny app provides live GBIF + GLOBI biodiversity network exploration relevant to Chapters 7 and 8: ```r shiny::runApp("shiny/bionexus/") ``` ## Package Analysis functions are collected in the **potytools** R package (`packages/potytools/`), being prepared for rOpenSci peer review. ## Project History | Date | Milestone | |---|---| | Feb 2026 | DNAzyme match first identified (`2025_toys/poty.qmd`) | | Mar 2026 | HGT analysis suite | | Apr 2026 | Codon usage analysis iterations | | May 2026 | Cleaned codon + DNAzyme notebook | | May 2026 | Final phylogenetic pipeline | | Jun 2026 | ELM motif & immunity notebooks; this document |
